item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gets an upvote from me, I see how many don't like it, but this is really up my alley and imo it fits in with the DoA canon, great job dude. +1
Well fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one!

The first half nicely built up the overall story of this little slice of hell and the second half made me certain that I never ever want to visit it!

Creepy, gnarly, gritty and leaves you thinking WTF just happened?!
Very well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This perfectly fits and did help develop my personal headcanon!

I LOVE IT!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Press F to pay respects

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That exploration log partially ruined the article for me to be honest. The moment with the button feels a little lolfoundation, and the death of those guys seemed like a very clichéd way to handle the exploration.

However, I really liked the first half and all those implications that came with it. It may not be a classic DoA, but it was interesting to read nonetheless.

So, overall, novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oof

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The grey entity grabs Γ-1 Taurus' chest with its free hand and pulls until his chest and waist are completely torn apart and separated.

WHY DID I HEAR THE ROBLOX DEATH SOUND?

+1

Deep into that darkness peering, long I stood there, wondering, fearing, doubting, dreaming dreams no mortal ever dared to dream before.

- Edgar Allan Poe, The Raven

Thanks for the support everyone. I really appreciate it, I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the exploration log was losing the clinical tone quite often in my opinion, and I'm not 100% I completely got the article, it was an enjoyable read.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think you quite understand the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen this done before, the "alternative foundation" is common and this version is ok but not deeply engaging. The scips they handled are ok and interesting in their own right. The exploration log doesn't work for me. I reads as a bunch of clueless redshirts where sent in, would have worked better if it was quickly trained D-class. I get the gag with the red button, of course you want to press it, but it just fails with the "elite" team. Nice start for your first SCP tho, keep at it! :)

item.title: Minor update
item.content:encoded: Changed the name for one of the things in the chambers since its name fits better for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to the Department of Abnormalities.
Chilling Ex. log, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did the researcher write those things in the notebook? Also how is that level 5 document related to the latter one? I feel like this needs more plot development. But other than that, the Department of Abnormalities reference was quite interesting. 0 (no vote) for now.
Holy hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have 4 major problems with this. 1st, it seems to miss the point of the Department of Abnormalities entirely. It puts far too human a face on things, tries to explain far too much and just doesn't have that feel of bizarre minimalism that defines what the DoA is. The DoA isn't some precursor organisation that existed before the Foundation or something like that, which seems to be what you're implying here (and if that isn't the case, I can't see how this fits the contest theme of history at all.)

2: You've got some fake anomalous item up front to disguise the existence of something that itself isn't even anomalous - some documents. So your actual SCP isn't even anomalous, and I don't get the reasoning behind the fake-out in the first place. If you wanted it restricted, you could just have a security check like the original DoA article.

3: The items themselves from the DoA docs just aren't very interesting. To be blunt, they all sound like something you'd see coldposted to the mainlist and quickly downvoted to deletion.

4: The first half of the exploration log seems to be filler - comms checks, going down the lift, re-describing all the objects you've previously given documents on etc. You could replace all of that with one sentence describing it and lose nothing. The 2nd half starts with them pushing a big red unlabelled humming button for some reason. That alone is hard enough to believe, but what follows just seems to be slaughter for the sake of slaughter. They went in there to investigate an unknown place, immediately pressed the off-switch for the containment systems and subsequently died.

-1 from me, I'm afraid.

Some other issues I noted:

Issues
– hide block

said uniform appears to be in a state of disrepair or heavily damaged state.

This is just stating the same thing twice.

the item immediately redshifts the being

Redshifting is a very specific term with a specific meaning and I don't think that's what you're describing here. What you are describing just sounds like turning them red.

Mobile Task Force Gamma-1 ("Search and Destroy")3 were sent en route to the location of the facility.

No need for "en route" here, it makes no sense.

Γ-1 Taurus shoots the individuals off of Γ-1 Pisces and picks him up.

You have a lot of lines with issues similar to this one but this stood out. Your description of the action here doesn't really convey what's happening very well. He shoots them with such force that they go flying off of Pisces?

(Γ-1 Sagittarius kneels over and picks up the unconscious Γ-1 Leo on his back.)

When did Leo become unconscious? He was screaming about being down the line before this.

item.title: Re: Provenance
item.content:encoded: Many images on Flickr are available under a suitable license. You have to check each one individually.

item.title: Image Removed
item.content:encoded: One of the images has been removed due to reasons stated above. I will replace it with another soon enough.

item.title: Re: Provenance
item.content:encoded: That's strange. I thought the site I got it from insured it was free to use.

item.title: Re: Provenance
item.content:encoded: How odd, I didn't see that. Anyway, the flowers are "all rights reserved", so you'll have to take them down.

item.title: Re: Provenance
item.content:encoded: I already credited the images in the first page. Unless there is an issue there?

item.title: Provenance
item.content:encoded:
elevator is from Pixabay, https://pixabay.com/en/elevator-fog-dark-incheon-985774/ (Creative Commons)

eyeless: no idea. Looks vaguely like someone's artwork. Did you make this yourself?

flowers: this looks to be based on a widely-circulated photo of a commercial product (it's a neon lamp made to look like flowers). Not CC, but replaceable.

gasmask: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:An_Air_Raid_Warden_wearing_his_steel_helmet_and_duty_gas_mask_during_the_Second_World_War._D4053.jpg (public domain)

many gasmasks: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:StateLibQld_2_202023_Soldiers_drill_in_their_gas_masks_during_World_War_I.jpg (public domain)

stairs: appear to be taken from one of those Free Wallpaper sites that all mirror each other. License unverifiable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in agreement with those disliking the way the Department of Abnormalities are presented. I don't think this really needs their name for it to be its own article, it's unsubtle and overlong in comparison.

That, and the fact that the documents themselves aren't inherently anomalous besides origin. It's not justified to be its own SCP, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed and added new dialogue, hopefully there aren't any problems now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some minor geographic problems in the second addendum:

Located in Russia, underneath the city of Pripyat.

Pripyat is in Ukraine.

Kiev Oblast, Border of the Chernobyl Exclusion Zone

Your text implies (at least to me) that the elevator, visible from the border of the exclusion zone, is pretty close to the main facility, underneath Pripyat. You may want to change this, as Pripyat is roughly in the center of the zone, which covers over 2000km squared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I haven't really gotten into the Department of Abnormalities, I feel your use here in which you change them from a rather faceless corner of the Foundation that contains the most cursed of objects into something more akin to the organization in Cabin in the Woods is a bit of a misstep. Gone is the mystery and in its place are rather run of the mill MTF logs and narmy letters. I would love for a more in-depth exploration of the DoA but this isn't the version of that which I feel would work, which I feel would need a little more fridge and intrigue rather than guns and explosions.

-1, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would make less sense if you haven't read 3790.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like to be critical to a new author. This just doesn't hook me, though—it feels extremely disjointed rather than a single narrative, and doesn't really explain why the majority of these files are O5 classified with a fake cover story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found it as an aesthetic on both Tumblr and Pinterest a little long before I joined and I imagined how cool this would look like as an SCP. Problem was, I wasn't really ready to join the site then, and I had a little trouble trying to explain it as an SCP.

+ Possible spoilers
- Hide

The story of #71839CA-102 revolves around two people. A girl named Lucy, and another guy who's her ex. You see, Lucy broke up with her ex for reasons unknown and went off to go with another man who just so happens to be her ex's brother.

Her ex was understandably pissed for it and decided to end their relationship right there and cut off all connections with his brother.

The thing is, her ex still loves her despite hating her for it.

So he goes to the local flowershop to give her some flowers and what happens after he buys the flowers is up to your personal interpretation of what happened next.

I based its premise off of the idea of love and hate together. The kind you get when you break up with someone, and you hate them for it, but deep down, you still miss them. You love them. This is literally based off of this weird sentence that was in my head: "I hate that I love you."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems pretty cool, so have a +1. #71839CA-102 reminds me of an artwork I once saw. Not remembering the name right now. What was your inspiration for that abnormality?

item.title: Another good one.
item.content:encoded: Ive found myself liking a lot of the entries so far. This one connects to a lesser known and decent scp so thats also a plus. its good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed it. Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Great, now djkaktus has two entries.

Just kidding. I remember this in draft form, I enjoyed then as I do now the origins aspect of the piece. It very much does justice to and continues the flavor of Gears’ proposal. I think IIRC in the draft I read, the MTF log ended with the recovery of the note and that was basically it. It’s more involved now for sure, and I think is an improvement. However, it did read like a scene from a FPS-shooter-to-movie adaptation. Having said that, imagery was unsettling for sure.

There are still some grammatical mistakes, another proofreading would hopefully reveal some. Here’s at least one, I’m sure I saw more:

The door is also rusted shut but the viewport is locked up. Preventing whatever is inside from being seen.

The second sentence is a fragment, the period needs to be a comma.

Also a conceptual note:

Γ-1 Sagittarius: Don't pull that! It's in your femoral artery, you'll bleed out!

I know not many enjoy commentary from specialists, but as a member of the medical field, I have to point out the amount of disbelief this inspires: it cannot be determined from gross anatomy that such a foreign body is currently in the artery. Where the femoral artery is most identifiable and superficial is also the site of a major vein and a major nerve, and they are all within the space of about 5 cm. (A quick google shows that the diameter of this artery is about 6 mm.) You would only know by radiology, a needled syringe, a thorough physical assessment of the distal limb, or the amount, color, and pulsitility of the blood loss after removal.

You can fix this by changing it to “It’s near your femoral artery, you could bleed out.” Still a good enough reason to leave it in.

The gas masked entity was truly unsettling. Overall, great work for a first SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't seen some good gore and violence in an article so I took it upon myself to bring it out here, Not everyone liked the MTF log though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip started slow with exposition but it gets better and better. I kind of liked the MTF log as it brings all the creatures previously mentioned into action and it's not everyday that you get to read such a gruesome spectacle like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, not everyone died in there. I know that not everyone likes it since, that's not how they imagined it to be, but I like the way I presented it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My biggest issue with this piece is how much of a face it puts on the Department of Abnormalities. I'm much more fond of their more subdued, quieter appearances in SCP-3790 and SCP-3220 because in those cases, it's more about what isn't said than what is. In this case, you're saying a lot about the Department, and not all of it sticks. The exploration log is also kind of cheesy, what with everyone dying gruesomely. It doesn't really click for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! While I'm not faring so well with some of the more established authors here, I can say that regardless, I'm quite proud that I managed to bring this out for my first article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good stuff, I always love seeing the sort of "prehistory" of the Foundation. +1, my dude

Oh my God.

Eta: Spoke too soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, I'll try to find a better image. In the meantime however, this one will have to stay.

ETA: Actually, SCP-2614 uses the same hallway image from SCP-398. I don't think that the same issue applies here (Especially since my version has been edited to make it look different.) although, I'll still find a better picture if I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean like, just to be clear: there's nothing at all that says you have to change, it's just that if I was told a photo I was using was already on the wiki, I myself would change out of personal preference. I think a fair number of other authors would agree with that sentiment, so I was really just trying to give you a heads up. There's nothing preventing you from using it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was told that, with the image severely edited, no one would make the connection. Unless its breaking some rule here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean… no? There's like no actual rule against it or anything, but you might want to change it given that it is already being used?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disregard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that the picture for #97165MA-066 has already been used in SCP-3440.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Spoilers
- I warned you.

Right, I forgot to add what's happening here. If you've figured it out already, good for you, but I just want to clear a couple things up.

What's happening here is, the Foundation discovered these documents and files. It's not stated where they got it, or how they even know of it, but they just found it.

When the Overseers heard about this, they send out an MTF to administer amnestics to the personnel involved in its discovery and confiscate the files. They log the files into the database in the form of a fake dangerous SCP and transfer the actual documents off to Site-01.

The fake skip is basically just a worn-out notepad with gibberish and doodles drawn in but its non-anomalous. They added security protocols to this fake skip so that, if one decides to steal or use it in unauthorized testing, they will know about it.

All in all, since the real SCP isn't anomalous to begin with, it's held in Site-01 as the only SCP to be brought into a Protected Site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The DOA is interesting chiefly because we don't really know what's going on (although honestly it's far from my favorite skip on the site, but those issues belong to that article). When it's spelled out like this, it lets all the air out - I don't think this benefits from exploration logs and documents (which I realize are the central point of this article). This isn't poorly executed, it's just conceptually misdirected - I'm actually looking forward to seeing you write more for the site. No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This simply didn't grab me at any point and a lot of it felt pretty cheesy, particularly the 'Fuck fuck fuck fuck' letter and all the stuff about how Shore is going to make that bastard Mulhausen pay for what he did. The MTF log itself felt like a slasher-horror pastiche, like a cut section of the action scenes at the end of 'Cabin in the Woods', or a draft for a Containment Breach sequel, and that seemed like a weak payoff given the overabundance of set-up. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, if you were grossed out by the body horror, I guess its safe to say that I've succeeded in that area.

By the way, I wanted to try and do a lot more with the Department of Abnormalities with this one. Since the last two were all Show and no Tell, I figured I'd try and build around the DoA with some worldbuilding.

It may break your headcanon for what you thought the DoA might be, but this is certainly what I wanted it to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still gonna novote. I liked it, but not enough to upvote. With the MTF log, I didn't feel creeped out, but rather thoroughly disgusted by the body horror. While this is normally good for an article, for a Department of Abnormalities article, I feel as though it throws away the original theme and tries to become its own thing.

However, I can't downvote. You did successfully execute the body horror, and the article was certainly an interesting read. Also I want my DoA logo to be canon.

That being said, good luck in the contest!

Edit: After further consideration, I've decided to downvote. This is essentially the anti-thesis of a DoA article, and would work infinitely better if it had DoA's name removed from it. Also I don't care about the logo anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I fixed it.

Actually, in canon, this takes place before the Foundation was still starting out. Like in Gears' proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my God, Thank you so much Ihp!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll give this a more thorough reading when I'm more awake, but I love the detail of the logo on the paper being aligned differently in each instance.

E: Y'know what, powered through sleep, read this, love this. Using Gears's 001 as the format for the abnormalities is genius, and I love the idea that these things are so wrong that even the Foundation is troubled by them on an existential level. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can’t vouch for the author, but I was under the upression the choice to favor the imperial system in the “DOA” documents was intentional. (To show an American basis and convay a general sense of ignorance to the outside world) Why on earth this USACentric rendition of the early Foundation is in Ukraine? I’ve no clue.

On a separate note I feel i’m Slowly falling in love with this “Deparmtmet of annomies” thing. It gives life to SCP’s “Jail for things that kill you” misconceived identify that I fell in love with in those strange February days so long ago.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This is my entry to the contest, as well as my first SCP to this site. I hope it bodes well with everyone.

+ Credits
- I can't thank these people enough.

YossiLeiner for making up most of my crit and making the DOA logo.
Dr Aers for crit.
Joreth for his review.
Mendelssohn for checking it out.
Zyn for looking at it.
Westrin for being Westrin.
TSATPWTCOTTTADC for improving the logo, improving the images, and helping me with the format and whatnot. You deserve a beer with me if I was old enough to drink.
djkaktus for being an inspiration.
And finally, The Great Hippo for not only crit, but being an even bigger inspiration. (sorry kaktus.)

Also, Big thanks to Flippsix on Discord for lending me their characters, Damian Noxol from SCP_RP!

+ Image Credits
- Hide

Gas Mask 1:Here.
Gas Mask 2:Here.
Eyelessedit: Here.
Flowers: Here.
Elevator: Here.
Stairwell: Here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just had a quick read through, here's a couple things I noticed;

You've got a few sentences which are redundant, like the following;-

All knowledge of SCP-4000's contents within its pages are the primary cause of infection.

All knowledge of SCP-4000's contents within its pages are the primary cause of infection.

And this;-

Uninfected subjects are capable of being afflicted with SCP-4000's effect by listening to their pleas and cries for help, or to listen to their dire situation.

It's a bit clunky to read in it's current form, and I think it'd be improved if you wrote something like;-

"SCP-4000's effect can be transferred to other individuals via an auditory memetic component - Hearing a test subjects vocalisations of distress will induce the same feelings of panic and distress in the subject."

Still not the best, but I think it's a slightly more scientific tone than what you've got, and it removes the redundancy at the end of the sentence. I'd suggest maybe calling people affected by SCP-4000 SCP-4000-1 or -alpha, something like that. Makes it easier if you need to later write about affected individuals or anything.

One other thing I noticed, in the additional documents at the bottom of the page you've put height & weight as feet and pounds. The Foundation uses the metric system, so i'd suggest changing this.

Good luck in the contest!
